title: Chapter 26: The Last Party
content: The invitation arrived on encrypted glass — a wafer-thin screen that dissolved after one reading. THE LAST SIGNAL — A Farewell to the Future. Black text on white light. No dress code. No plus-one limit. No ambiguity about what "last" meant.
Two thousand people RSVP'd in the first hour.

I stand inside Cliff House now, camera pressed to my eye, and I understand why. Jax Vance is throwing her funeral and calling it a party, and everyone — the investors, the socialites, the tech aristocracy, the beautiful and the ruthless — they all want to watch her go. Some to mourn. Some to gloat. Most to say they were there when it happened.

The hologram system turns the Pacific into a canvas. Whales breach in slow motion above the dance floor, their bodies translucent and vast, trailing ribbons of bioluminescent code. Constellations rotate across the ceiling — not the real ones, but the ones Jax designed, star patterns that spell out equations in a language only she reads. And then the code begins to fall, cascading from the rafters in sheets of green and gold, digital rain that moves like weather, like something alive.

It pours over the crowd. It pours over me. I feel it on my skin — not wet, not cold, but warm, like standing in sunlight filtered through stained glass. The code paints everyone in flickering light, and for a moment the whole room looks like a cathedral made of data.
This is the most spectacular party Jax has ever thrown.
I film everything.

My camera is a ghost — tiny, stabilized, wireless. Jax gave it to me three days ago along with the address of a decentralized server she'd set up, nodes spread across twelve jurisdictions, encrypted with a key that only I hold. "After tonight," she said, "this footage can't be subpoenaed. It can't be deleted. It can't be owned by anyone but you." She looked at me then, and something in her expression was already gone — already packing, already boarding a flight that doesn't exist on any manifest.
I'm not making a documentary anymore.
I'm preserving evidence of a life.

The bass vibrates through the floor, through the soles of my feet, up through my shins and into my chest until my heartbeat syncs with the rhythm. The DJ is someone famous, someone I should probably recognize, but I don't look at the booth. I look at the faces. I capture the way champagne glasses catch the holographic light, the way the bubbles rise like tiny prayers. I capture the woman laughing with her head thrown back, the man crying into his cufflink, the couple arguing in the corner with the desperate intensity of people who know the world is ending and can't agree on how to face it.

I smell salt air cutting through the perfume and the sweat — the Pacific, relentless and eternal, shoving its way into this temple of impermanence. The cliff House is built on the edge of the world, and tonight the ocean sounds like it's breathing.
And then Vivienne walks in.

My camera drops. I catch it, but for one full second I am not filming. I am just standing here, a person with a racing heart and a dry mouth, watching the most beautiful woman I have ever seen walk into a room.

She's wearing Mugler. A backless gown, black and gold, the architecture of it fierce and deliberate — sharp shoulders, a nipped waist, a skirt that moves like liquid metal. The gold traces her spine like a circuit board, like a map of some country I've never visited but want to explore. Her hair is pulled back tight, exposing the graceful architecture of her neck, the strong line of her jaw.

This is not the society wife who stood beside Tom Hargrove at charity galas, smiling on cue. This is not the girl from Akron who flew to San Francisco with nothing but a scholarship and a hunger. This is someone new. Someone who chose.
She walks through the party like she owns it.

And in a way, she does. Every inch of this — the house, the holograms, the champagne, the two thousand people pretending they're not here to watch a empire end — it was all built for her. Jax Vance didn't create a company. She created an offering. A temple. And Vivienne Sterling is the goddess it was raised for.
I lift my camera and film her. Not because it's evidence. Because the world should see what it looks like when a woman becomes herself.

I find the cliff edge an hour later, or maybe it finds me. The party is a sound and a glow behind me, the bass now a distant pulse, the laughter blurred by wind and distance. I sit with my legs dangling over the drop, the camera in my lap, and I breathe.
The stars are close tonight. No fog, no marine layer, no interference. Just the Milky Way spread across the sky like a bruise, like a love letter, like code that wrote itself.
"Mind if I sit?"

Jax lowers herself beside me. She's wearing a tuxedo — classic, minimal, perfectly tailored. Her hair is down for once, and the wind catches it, and she looks younger than I've ever seen her. She looks like someone who might have been my friend in another life, in a life where neither of us was chasing or running or building empires.
We sit in silence for a moment. The waves crash far below us, and I can taste the salt on my lips.
"Thank you," Jax says.
I turn to look at her. "For what?"

She's staring out at the ocean, not at me. The holographic code has followed us somehow — faint lines of it drift in the air, painting her skin in shifting gold. "For seeing me. Not the myth. Not the algorithm. Me."
My throat tightens. "You're thanking me?"
Jax turns then, and her eyes are bright, not with tears — I don't think Jax Vance cries, I don't think she remembers how — but with something rawer. Something unsimulated.
"You're the only person who ever asked questions instead of making assumptions," she says. "That's rarer than genius. Rarer than money."

I don't know what to say. I've interviewed billionaires and whistleblowers, I've faced down lawyers and lobbyists, but this — Jax Vance sitting on a cliff, thanking me for seeing her, like that was a gift and not just the bare minimum of human decency — this undoes me.
"I was doing my job," I manage.
Jax smiles. A real one, crooked and brief. "No, Maya. You were doing something much more dangerous. You were paying attention."
The bass shifts inside the house — a slower song, something with strings and silence between the notes. Jax hears it and tilts her head, like she's listening to a frequency I can't perceive.
"They're going to play our song," she says, and stands, and brushes the dust from her tuxedo, and walks back toward the light.
I stay on the cliff edge. I breathe. The stars don't say anything back.
"Maya."
Jordan's voice comes from the dark. I spin, camera up by instinct, already filming.

They look terrible. Pale, shaking, their hands jammed into the pockets of a jacket that's too big for them — borrowed, stolen, I can't tell. Their eyes are red-rimmed and enormous, and they're breathing in short bursts, like they've been running.
"I'm being subpoenaed." The words tumble out. "Tom's lawyer — Whitfield — they know about the device. They know I planted it. I'm going to be charged as an accessory."
I lower the camera slowly. "When?"

"Tonight. Before midnight. There's a process server in the driveway — I saw them when I came in." Jordan's hand emerges from their pocket, and they're holding a folded document, and even in the dark I can see their fingers trembling. The paper looks cold. It looks like it was cut from something living. "They haven't found me yet. They will."
I reach out and touch their arm. They flinch, then steady. "What do you need?"

Jordan looks at me, and for the first time since I've known them — since I've been filming them, watching them, trying to understand them — they are not performing. There is no calculation behind their eyes. No angle. Just fear, and underneath it, something that might be desperation, and underneath that, something that might be the truth.
"I need Jax to make me disappear too."
I don't answer immediately. The wind fills the silence. The bass from the party is a heartbeat I can feel in my teeth.
"Why would she help you?" I ask. "You planted the device. You're the reason any of this happened."

Jordan flinches again, harder this time, but they don't look away. "Because I have information that will keep her out of prison. Tom's communications — the real ones, not the ones he deleted. I have copies. Everything. The wire transfers. The NDAs. The threats. All of it."
I stare at them. "You've been sitting on evidence this whole time?"

"I was protecting myself." Their voice cracks. "I was always protecting myself. That's all I've ever done. And now —" They laugh, and it's the worst sound I've ever heard, a laugh with no joy in it at all. "And now I'm standing on a cliff asking the woman I betrayed to save my life."
"That's not why she'd help you," I say, though I don't know if I believe it.
"Then why?"

I look at Jordan — really look, past the trembling and the terror and the self-interest that's been their armor for so long — and I see it. The thing they've been hiding under all those layers of calculation and survival.
"Because," I say slowly, "she's the closest thing you have to a friend."
Jordan's face collapses. Not into tears — something worse. Into recognition. They hear their own truth spoken aloud, and it breaks them open like a shell.
"Yeah," they whisper. "I know it sounds insane. But yeah."

I take their hand. The subpoena paper is still crumpled in their other fist, and I can feel the cold of it, the institutional weight, the way it turns a person into a case number. I've felt that cold before.
"Come on," I say. "Let's go find her."
Inside, the party has transformed.

The holographic rain is everywhere now — not just falling from the ceiling, but rising from the floor, drifting sideways, spiraling in slow vortices that catch the light and scatter it across every surface. The code reflects on skin, on glass, on the wet rims of abandoned champagne flutes. It turns the room into a snow globe of data, and everyone inside it is glittering, incandescent, briefly immortal.
I see Jax and Vivienne in the center of the dance floor.

The music has slowed to something almost silent — a pulse, a breath, a chord sustained beyond reason. And they are dancing. Not performing, not for the cameras that circle them like satellites, not for the crowd that has parted to watch. They are dancing for each other.

Vivienne's hands rest on Jax's shoulders. Jax's hands frame Vivienne's waist. They are close — foreheads almost touching, eyes locked, moving in a small, slow circle like the last two objects in a collapsing solar system. The holographic code falls through them, around them, between them. It paints Vivienne's Mugler gown in cascading gold. It catches in Jax's hair like digital snow.
I lift my camera. My hands are steady. This is what I do. I preserve.
But I am also crying. I don't know when it started. The tears blur the viewfinder, and I blink them away, and I film, and I cry, and I film.
This is the most beautiful thing I have ever seen.

Two women holding each other, moving slowly while the digital world dissolves around them. Vivienne's eyes are closed, her lips curved in a smile that is not for anyone but the woman in her arms. Jax is watching her — watching Vivienne like she's memorizing her, like she's encoding her into something permanent, something that can't be deleted or subpoenaed or burned.

The code drifts down between them. Golden lines of it land on Vivienne's bare back, tracing her spine like the gown does, like a second skin made of light. Jax's fingers follow the code — unconsciously, instinctively — and Vivienne shivers, and Jax pulls her closer, and the code wraps around them both like a cocoon, like an ending, like a beginning.

After tonight, "Jax Vance" will cease to exist. The name, the company, the myth — all of it will dissolve into the same digital rain that's falling now. The algorithm will be deployed. The exit strategy will execute. The woman in the tuxedo will vanish, and what's left will be something quieter, something without a stock ticker or a Forbes profile or a price per share.

But in this moment — in the holographic rain, in the warmth of code on skin, in the slow rotation of two people who chose each other against all reason and all probability — she is the most real person in the room.
The most real person I have ever filmed.

I hold the shot. I don't move. The party continues around them — the two thousand witnesses, the champagne, the whales still breaching in ghostly silence above the crowd — but none of it matters. Only this. Only the two of them, turning slowly in a storm of light, holding on like letting go would mean falling forever.

Jordan stands beside me, watching. Their subpoena has vanished — stuffed back into a pocket, maybe, or dropped, or simply forgotten in the face of something that makes legal documents look like what they are: paper. Just paper.
"Is that what love looks like?" Jordan asks quietly.
I keep filming. My voice is steady, even though nothing else is.
"That's what it looks like when it's true."

The code falls. The music holds. And somewhere between one heartbeat and the next, Jax Vance — the myth, the algorithm, the genius, the ghost — tips her head back and laughs. A real laugh, full and startled and alive, like she's surprised by her own happiness. Like she didn't expect to feel it. Like she didn't think she'd be allowed.
Vivienne pulls her close again, and the code closes around them like a curtain, like a door, like the end of a story that is also a beginning.
I film until the last light falls.
And then I stop. And I save. And I hold the evidence of a life in my hands — warm and irreducible and impossible to delete — and I carry it with me into whatever comes next.
